rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF RICHARD MARSHALL DIXON
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer To Petitioner Aaron R. Granquists Probate Code Section 850 Petition For Claim Of Adverse Possession (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] Petitioner alleges that Elinor's interest in the real property automatically vested and she became a tenant in common with any other intestate heirs to Richard's estate upon his death. Assuming, arguendo, that she possessed the property as a tenant in common as opposed to a holdover conservator, the issue of hostile and exclusive possession is problematic. Although the petition makes the conclusory statement that Elinor's possession and occupation of the real property was hostile and exclusive, the material facts properly pleaded on the face of the petition do not show that Elinor asserted a hostile and exclusive claim to the real property, as opposed to merely a joint possessor with other tenants in common. The law requires that the possession be made in such a way as to put the other intestate heirs or tenants in common on notice of hostility. As stated in Johns v. Scobie (1939) 12 Cal. 2d at 623-25, "It is a fundamental rule that each tenant in common has a right to occupy the whole of the property. The possession of one is deemed the possession of all; each may assume that another in exclusive possession is possessing for all and not adversely to the others; and consequently one tenant in common does not, merely by exclusive possession, gain title by adverse possession against the others. Such possession will be presumed to be by permission and rightful, unless notice is brought home to the others that it has become hostile." As reinforced in Wood v. Davidson (1944) 62 Cal. App. 2d 885, 890, "the adverse user, must unfurl his flag on the land, and keep it flying so that the owner may see, if he will, that an enemy has invaded his domains, and planted the standard of conquest." The Petition fails to plead facts sufficient to establish this element of adverse possession. A demurrer is not the proper vehicle to address the allegations related to the failure to object to, or appeal from, the Court's May 14, 2019 Order Confirming Sale. Respondent shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on January 6, 2021. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. The appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 2 of 2]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF RICHARD MARSHALL DIXON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer To Petitioner Aaron R. Granquists Probate Code Section 850 Petition For Claim Of Adverse Possession
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Respondent's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED, and the Objections to Judicial Notice are OVERRULED. (See Evid. Code sections 452, 453.) The Court will take judicial notice only of relevant facts properly subject to notice in the materials presented and will not take notice of hearsay, or assertions of fact, in the court-filed materials. Respondent's Demurrer to Petition Pursuant to Probate Code Section 850 for Claim of Adverse Possession is SUSTAINED with leave to amend, pursuant to CCP section 430.10(e). For the purpose of testing the sufficiency of the cause of action, the Court considers all material facts properly pleaded in the Petition as true. In an action to quiet title based on adverse possession, the claimant must plead and prove five elements: (1) Possession must be by actual occupation under such circumstances as to constitute reasonable notice to the owner. (2) It must be hostile and exclusive to the owner's title. (3) The holder must claim the property as his own under either color of title or claim of right. (4) Possession must be continuous and uninterrupted for five years. (5) The holder must pay all the taxes levied and assessed upon the property during the period. (Dimmick v. Dimmick (1962) 58 Cal. 2d 417, 421.) In attempting to plead sufficient facts to show each element of adverse possession, the Petition fails to acknowledge or address Elinor Dixon's continuing fiduciary duties as the court-appointed conservator of her brother's estate. As a matter of law, the death of a conservatee does not extinguish a conservator's duties and obligations, nor does it "terminate the probate court's jurisdiction over the conservatorship estate." (Conservatorship of O'Connor (1996) 48 Cal. App. 4th 1076, 1088.) Rather, although the "death of a conservatee terminates the conservatorship, the conservator has a continuing "duty of custody and conservation of the estate" after the conservatee's death "pending the delivery thereof to the personal representative of the ... conservatee's estate or other disposition according to law." (Prob. Code, section 2467, subd. (a).)" (Id., at p. 1089.) Following the death of conservatee Richard's death, Elinor's "duty of custody and conservation was continuing pending some disposition by the court." (Ibid.) The Petition fails to plead facts showing that Elinor submitted a final accounting in the conservatorship estate, or that the Probate Court relieved her of her obligations as conservator. It also fails to plead facts showing that Elinor performed her obligation to deliver the conservatorship estate to Richard's personal representative, or that any administration of Richard's estate was undertaken. Because she obtained possession of the property as a conservator, and she was never relieved of her obligations as a conservator by the Probate Court, the Petition has failed to plead that she held the property under color of title or claim of right. [Part 1 of 2]
